topic: Introduction to Cardiology / Investigations of the Cardiac System

cards.q: Define investigations in cardiology.
cards.a: Tests or procedures for evaluation of cardiovascular diseases.

cards.q: Where are cardiological investigations carried out?
cards.a: Either at the bedside and/or in laboratories.

cards.q: List the seven objectives of investigations in cardiology.
cards.a: To (1) establish the diagnosis, (2) determine the aetiology, (3) identify the risk factors, (4) detect the complications, (5) monitor the disease progression, (6) evaluate associated co-morbidities, and (7) make prognosis.

cards.q: List the twelve investigations in cardiology.
cards.a: Chest X-ray; electrocardiogram; electrophysiological studies; echocardiography; cardiac MRI; cardiac catheterization; coronary arteriography; cardiac enzymes and troponins; brain natriuretic peptide; lipid profiles; blood sugar; and electrolytes, urea and creatinine.

cards.q: Define non-invasive investigations and give examples.
cards.a: Procedures or tests limited to the body surface — e.g. standard 12-lead ECG and transthoracic echocardiography.

cards.q: Define invasive investigations and give examples.
cards.a: Procedures or tests that involve penetration of the vascular system or body tissues — e.g. electrophysiological studies, cardiac catheterization, transoesophageal echocardiography and angiography.

cards.q: What does a chest X-ray utilize, and what is it mainly useful for in cardiology?
cards.a: It utilizes ionizing radiation called X-ray; it is mainly useful in the study of cardiac structure and changes in the pulmonary circulation which may be of cardiac origin.

cards.q: List the uses of chest X-ray in cardiology.
cards.a: To diagnose cardiomegaly; to identify features of heart diseases such as hypertension, heart failure, cardiomyopathy, congenital heart diseases, pericardial effusion, coarctation of aorta, and valvular and pericardial calcifications; to diagnose acute pulmonary oedema; and to identify precipitants of heart failure e.g. chest infection.

cards.q: State the two chest X-ray criteria for diagnosis of cardiomegaly in adults.
cards.a: Cardiothoracic ratio (CTR) greater than 50%, and maximum transverse cardiac diameter greater than 15.5 cm.

cards.q: Define the cardiothoracic ratio.
cards.a: The ratio of the maximum transverse cardiac diameter to the maximum internal diameter of the thoracic cage, expressed as a percentage.

cards.q: Define the electrocardiogram.
cards.a: The graphical record of the electrical activities of the heart obtained at the body surface.

cards.q: Describe the standard 12-lead ECG.
cards.a: It utilizes 12 leads, is recorded at rest over a period of 10–20 seconds, and is the most widely used form of ECG.

cards.q: What is Holter monitoring, and for how long does it record?
cards.a: Ambulatory ECG — it records the ECG for 24 hours.

cards.q: What is a stress ECG?
cards.a: An ECG recorded during exercise.

cards.q: Give the chamber and rhythm indications for ECG.
cards.a: Chamber abnormalities e.g. ventricular hypertrophy and left atrial abnormalities; cardiac arrhythmias; heart blocks (atrioventricular and bundle branch blocks); pre-excitation syndromes e.g. Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome; and cardiac arrest.

cards.q: Give the myocardial and pericardial indications for ECG.
cards.a: Ischaemic heart diseases, myocarditis, cardiomyopathies, pericardial diseases, and congenital heart diseases.

cards.q: Give the extracardiac indications for ECG.
cards.a: Electrolyte derangement e.g. hyperkalaemia and hypokalaemia; drug effect e.g. digoxin toxicity; pulmonary embolism; and cor pulmonale.

cards.q: What are electrophysiological studies used for?
cards.a: They are invasive procedures for studying cardiac arrhythmias and pre-excitation syndromes such as Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome.

cards.q: Define echocardiography.
cards.a: Cardiac imaging using ultrasound; it is otherwise known as cardiac ultrasound.

cards.q: Describe transthoracic echocardiography.
cards.a: It utilizes a transthoracic probe or transducer to obtain cardiac imaging from the body surface.

cards.q: Describe transoesophageal echocardiography.
cards.a: It utilizes a transoesophageal probe or transducer to obtain cardiac images very close to the heart.

cards.q: List the uses of the echocardiogram.
cards.a: To evaluate cardiac structures, functions and dysfunctions; to establish diagnosis of some cardiovascular diseases; and to detect complications of cardiovascular diseases.

cards.q: List the ten indications for echocardiogram.
cards.a: Hypertensive heart diseases; congenital heart diseases; rheumatic heart diseases; valvular heart diseases; infective endocarditis; cardiomyopathies; pericardial diseases especially pericardial effusion; ischaemic heart disease; heart failure; and cardiac masses e.g. intramural thrombus and atrial myxoma.

cards.q: How does cardiac MRI work?
cards.a: It utilizes a powerful magnetic field to align the nuclear magnetization of hydrogen atoms in water in the body.

cards.q: State two key facts about cardiac MRI.
cards.a: It does not use ionizing radiation, and it is the current gold standard for evaluation of left ventricular hypertrophy. It is useful in the study of cardiac structure.

cards.q: Describe cardiac catheterization.
cards.a: An invasive procedure involving introduction of a catheter through the veins into the heart under fluoroscopy guidance.

cards.q: What is cardiac catheterization useful for?
cards.a: Evaluation of intracardiac pressures; and it may also serve therapeutic value in the management of valvular lesions or congenital heart diseases such as atrial or ventricular septal defects.

cards.q: Describe coronary arteriography and its use.
cards.a: An invasive procedure which involves the use of contrast agents for evaluation of the coronary arteries; it is useful in the diagnosis and management of coronary artery diseases.

cards.q: What are cardiac enzymes and troponins, and when are they elevated?
cards.a: They are markers of myocardial injury, and are elevated in myocardial infarction.

cards.q: Name the three cardiac enzymes.
cards.a: Creatine phosphokinase (CK), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), and lactate dehydrogenase (LDH).

cards.q: Name the troponins.
cards.a: Troponin I and troponin T.

cards.q: What is BNP, and where is it produced?
cards.a: A 32-amino acid polypeptide produced by the ventricles.

cards.q: What is NT-proBNP?
cards.a: A 76 amino acid N-terminal fragment polypeptide co-secreted with BNP.

cards.q: What are BNP and NT-proBNP useful for?
cards.a: Both are useful in the screening and diagnosis of heart failure, and they are very useful in establishing prognosis of heart failure.

cards.q: What is the purpose of the other, supportive investigations?
cards.a: They are useful in detecting risk factors, complications or co-morbidities.

cards.q: List the components of the fasting lipid profile.
cards.a: Total cholesterol (TC), low density lipoprotein (LDL), high density lipoprotein (HDL), and triglycerides (TG).

cards.q: Why is blood sugar measured, and which tests are used?
cards.a: To exclude the presence or not of diabetes mellitus; using fasting blood sugar (FBS) and 2-hour postprandial sugar (2HPPS).

cards.q: Which electrolytes are measured in cardiological assessment?
cards.a: Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺ and HCO₃⁻.

cards.q: Why are urea and creatinine analysed in cardiological assessment?
cards.a: (1) To exclude effects of electrolytes as being responsible for the cardiovascular dysfunction; and (2) to detect whether or not renal disease is present in the patient.

cards.q: OSPE — Before commenting on heart size on a chest X-ray, what three checks must you make?
cards.a: Is it PA or AP (heart size can only be assessed on PA); is the patient rotated (medial ends of both clavicles equidistant from the spinous processes); and is inspiration adequate (5–7 anterior ribs visible above the diaphragm).

cards.q: OSPE — Why can cardiomegaly not be diagnosed on an AP chest film?
cards.a: Because the heart lies anteriorly and is therefore far from the film in an AP projection, so it is magnified by the diverging X-ray beam.

cards.q: OSPE — Between which two points is the maximum transverse cardiac diameter measured?
cards.a: From the most rightward point of the right heart border to the most leftward point of the left heart border — the two points are usually at different heights.

cards.q: OSPE — When measuring the thoracic diameter for the CTR, do you measure to the inner or outer rib edge?
cards.a: The INNER edge — it is the maximum INTERNAL diameter of the thoracic cage.

cards.q: OSPE — State the standard ECG paper speed and calibration, and what one small square represents.
cards.a: 25 mm/s and 10 mm/mV; one small square = 0.04 s horizontally and 0.1 mV vertically, and one large square = 0.20 s.

cards.q: OSPE — How do you calculate the rate on a REGULAR ECG rhythm?
cards.a: 300 divided by the number of large squares between two R waves — giving the sequence 300, 150, 100, 75, 60, 50.

cards.q: OSPE — How do you calculate the rate on an IRREGULAR rhythm?
cards.a: Count the QRS complexes on the 10-second rhythm strip and multiply by 6.

cards.q: OSPE — State the systematic sequence for reading a 12-lead ECG.
cards.a: Patient details and calibration; rate; rhythm; axis; P waves; PR interval; QRS; ST segment; T waves; QT interval; then summary and conclusion.

cards.q: OSPE — Using leads I and aVF, how do you determine the axis?
cards.a: Both upright = normal axis; lead I up with aVF down = left axis deviation; lead I down with aVF up = right axis deviation; both down = extreme axis.

cards.q: OSPE — Which ECG leads correspond to the inferior, anterior and lateral territories?
cards.a: Inferior: II, III and aVF. Anterior: V1–V4. Lateral: I, aVL, V5 and V6.

cards.q: OSPE — An ECG is irregularly irregular with absent P waves and a fine fibrillatory baseline. Diagnosis?
cards.a: Atrial fibrillation.

cards.q: OSPE — Why are P waves absent in atrial fibrillation?
cards.a: Because hundreds of chaotic atrial circuits fire simultaneously, so there is no single organised atrial depolarisation to produce a P wave — only a fibrillatory baseline.

cards.q: OSPE — Why is the ventricular rhythm irregular in atrial fibrillation?
cards.a: Because the AV node blocks most of the chaotic atrial impulses and conducts others at random, so the ventricles are stimulated at random intervals.

cards.q: OSPE — In a patient with atrial fibrillation, why is an echocardiogram requested?
cards.a: To look for structural heart disease, valvular disease (especially rheumatic mitral stenosis), and atrial thrombus — cardiac masses such as intramural thrombus being a listed echo indication.

cards.q: OSPE — An ECG shows tall tented T waves, flattened P waves and a widening QRS in a patient with chronic kidney disease. Diagnosis?
cards.a: Hyperkalaemia — a medical emergency which may progress to a sine wave pattern and cardiac arrest.

cards.q: OSPE — State the sequence of ECG changes as potassium rises.
cards.a: Tall tented T waves; flattening then loss of P waves; lengthening PR interval; widening QRS; QRS merging with T to form a sine wave; then ventricular fibrillation or asystole.

cards.q: OSPE — How do you distinguish a hyperkalaemic T wave from other tall T waves?
cards.a: The hyperkalaemic T wave is narrow-based, pointed and symmetrical — 'tented' like a church spire — whereas other tall T waves are broad-based.

cards.q: OSPE — What is the order of treatment in hyperkalaemia, and what does each step do?
cards.a: Stabilise, shift, remove: calcium gluconate stabilises the myocardium (it does not lower potassium); insulin with dextrose and salbutamol shift potassium into cells; dialysis or excretion removes it from the body.

cards.q: OSPE — What ECG changes occur in HYPOkalaemia?
cards.a: Flattened T waves, appearance of U waves, and ST depression; with risk of torsades de pointes.

cards.q: OSPE — On an ECG the P waves are regular but bear no relationship to the QRS complexes, which are wide and slow. Diagnosis?
cards.a: Complete (third degree) atrioventricular heart block, with a ventricular escape rhythm.

cards.q: OSPE — Why does a WIDE QRS in complete heart block indicate a more dangerous situation?
cards.a: Because a wide QRS indicates a ventricular escape rhythm (20–40/min), which is slower and less reliable than a narrow-complex junctional escape (40–60/min) and may fail, causing asystole.

cards.q: OSPE — Why must you look for inferior myocardial infarction in a patient with complete heart block?
cards.a: Because in most people the right coronary artery supplies both the inferior wall and the AV node, so occlusion causes inferior infarction and heart block together.

cards.q: OSPE — How do you distinguish first degree, Mobitz I, Mobitz II and complete heart block?
cards.a: First degree: PR > 0.20 s but every P conducts. Mobitz I: PR progressively lengthens then a beat is dropped. Mobitz II: PR constant then a beat is suddenly dropped. Complete: no relationship at all between P waves and QRS complexes.

cards.q: OSPE — ST elevation in leads II, III and aVF. What is the diagnosis and which artery?
cards.a: Acute inferior ST-elevation myocardial infarction, usually due to occlusion of the right coronary artery.

cards.q: OSPE — In which leads would you look for reciprocal changes in an inferior myocardial infarction?
cards.a: Leads I and aVL.

cards.q: OSPE — How does the ST elevation of pericarditis differ from that of myocardial infarction?
cards.a: Pericarditis causes widespread, saddle-shaped ST elevation with NO reciprocal depression, whereas infarction causes territorial ST elevation with reciprocal depression in the opposite leads.

cards.q: OSPE — Should you wait for the troponin result before treating a STEMI?
cards.a: No — the ECG diagnosis is sufficient. Troponin takes hours to rise, and myocardium is dying meanwhile. Troponin confirms; the ECG decides.

cards.q: OSPE — Why are troponins preferred to CK, AST and LDH?
cards.a: Because troponin I and T are essentially specific to heart muscle, whereas CK is also in skeletal muscle, AST also in liver, and LDH in almost every tissue.

cards.q: OSPE — An ECG shows a short PR interval with a slurred upstroke of the QRS. What is this?
cards.a: A delta wave — indicating Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome, a pre-excitation syndrome.

cards.q: OSPE — An ECG shows downsloping ST depression with a 'reverse tick' appearance. What is the likely cause?
cards.a: Digoxin effect — drug effect being a listed indication for ECG.

cards.q: OSPE — On an echocardiogram there is a large black echo-free space surrounding the heart. What is it, and why is it black?
cards.a: A pericardial effusion; fluid appears black on ultrasound because it does not reflect the ultrasound beam.

cards.q: OSPE — On echocardiography, what finding indicates that a pericardial effusion has caused tamponade?
cards.a: Collapse of the right-sided chambers, which are thin-walled and low-pressure and therefore compress first.

cards.q: OSPE — Why does cardiac tamponade cause shock?
cards.a: Because raised pericardial pressure prevents the chambers FILLING, so stroke volume and cardiac output fall — it impairs filling rather than squeezing the pump.

cards.q: OSPE — Why are IV fluids given, and diuretics avoided, in cardiac tamponade?
cards.a: Because the problem is inadequate filling; fluids raise filling pressure and help chambers resist collapse, whereas diuretics and vasodilators reduce filling pressure and worsen it.

cards.q: OSPE — What is Beck's triad?
cards.a: Hypotension, raised JVP and muffled heart sounds — the classic triad of cardiac tamponade.

cards.q: OSPE — Give the chest X-ray appearance suggesting pericardial effusion rather than cardiomegaly.
cards.a: A globular, 'water-bottle' shaped cardiac shadow with CLEAR lung fields — whereas heart failure gives a big heart with wet lungs. Only echocardiography distinguishes them definitively.

cards.q: OSPE — What is the earliest chest X-ray sign of left heart failure?
cards.a: Upper lobe venous diversion — the upper zone vessels become as large as or larger than the lower zone vessels, reversing the normal pattern.

cards.q: OSPE — What does blunting of the costophrenic angles indicate, and what does bilateral blunting suggest?
cards.a: A pleural effusion; bilateral blunting suggests a systemic cause such as heart failure rather than a local lung problem.

cards.q: OSPE — Give the mnemonic for the chest X-ray features of heart failure.
cards.a: ABCDE — Alveolar oedema, kerley B lines, Cardiomegaly, upper lobe Diversion, and pleural Effusions.

cards.q: CLINICAL — A patient complains of intermittent palpitations but the 12-lead ECG is normal. What investigation is indicated and why?
cards.a: Holter or ambulatory ECG, which records for 24 hours — because a standard ECG records only 10–20 seconds and will miss an intermittent arrhythmia.

cards.q: CLINICAL — A patient has chest pain only on exertion, with a normal resting ECG. What investigation is indicated and why?
cards.a: A stress ECG, recorded during exercise — because at rest the coronary supply is adequate, and only increased demand unmasks the ischaemia.

cards.q: CLINICAL — A breathless patient may have cardiac or respiratory disease. Which blood test helps most, and how?
cards.a: BNP or NT-proBNP — a normal level makes heart failure unlikely, so attention shifts to the lungs.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Why must a patient's renal function be known before coronary arteriography?
cards.a: Because the procedure uses contrast agents, which are nephrotoxic; and urea and creatinine are analysed to detect whether renal disease is present.

cards.q: CLINICAL — In suspected infective endocarditis, why may transoesophageal echocardiography be needed?
cards.a: Because vegetations are small, and the transoesophageal probe images the heart very close up, giving far better resolution than the transthoracic view.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Why is a fasting sample required for a lipid profile?
cards.a: Because triglycerides rise sharply after a meal, which falsely raises the TG and distorts the calculated LDL; cholesterol changes little with food.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Which lipid fraction is harmful and which is protective, and why?
cards.a: LDL is harmful because it carries cholesterol from the liver to the tissues including artery walls; HDL is protective because it carries cholesterol from the tissues back to the liver.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Why may a diabetic patient have a myocardial infarction without chest pain?
cards.a: Because diabetic neuropathy damages the nerves carrying cardiac pain, producing silent myocardial infarction.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Why are FBS and 2HPPS both used rather than one alone?
cards.a: FBS detects those whose baseline sugar is already raised; 2HPPS detects those with a normal fasting sugar who cannot handle a glucose load — an earlier stage the FBS would miss.

cards.q: CLINICAL — A patient presents with a dangerous arrhythmia. Why must electrolytes be checked before assuming heart disease?
cards.a: Because abnormal potassium or calcium alone causes arrhythmias in a structurally normal heart — electrolytes must be excluded as being responsible for the cardiovascular dysfunction.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Why does heart failure require both BNP and echocardiography?
cards.a: BNP answers whether it is heart failure; echocardiography answers what kind — reduced or preserved ejection fraction — which determines treatment.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Why is the ECG the most valuable investigation in acute cardiology?
cards.a: Because it is instant, bedside and non-invasive, whereas laboratory tests take time.

cards.q: CLINICAL — A patient has an ECG showing electrical activity but no palpable pulse. What does this illustrate?
cards.a: That the ECG records electricity only and not pumping — this is pulseless electrical activity, a cardiac arrest rhythm.
